title: Walk into the adds meeting already prepared
kicker: A ranked prep list, local artists flagged — you still make the call.
youWillNeed: Claude Code installed and signed in (do the 30-minute walkthrough first if not)
A folder on your machine called station-work
The practice file below — do NOT use a real adds list while you're learning
guardrail: Adds are a human editorial call, every time. Claude gathers, ranks, and flags — it never gets to recommend what actually goes into rotation, and nothing here replaces someone actually listening to the music.

sampleData.description: A fake adds list: 12 candidate tracks, 6 of them local, with release dates and spin counts. Genre tags in this practice file are deliberately unreliable, which is part of the lesson.

steps.title: Ask for a ranked prep list, not a decision
steps.do: Point Claude at the spreadsheet and ask for a talking order for the meeting, with local artists flagged, and be explicit that it should not recommend actual adds.
steps.prompt: There is one spreadsheet in this folder — candidates for Tuesday's adds meeting. Read it, then give me a ranked list for the meeting: which tracks should we talk about first. Flag every local artist clearly, since those get priority in our rotation policy. Don't recommend actually adding anything — that's a human call in the meeting — just help me walk in prepared.
steps.youWillSee: Claude will ask your permission to read the file. Say yes. It will then read all 12 rows and hand back a ranked talking order with every local artist marked, plus a short note on anything in the data it thinks is worth questioning before you rely on it.

steps.title: Read the caution before the ranking
steps.do: Look at what Claude flagged about the data itself before you look at the ranking.
steps.youWillSee: In this recording, Claude leads with the caution before it says anything else: this is fake data, and it names the placeholder artist names as the tell. Further down, it flags two things worth raising in the meeting: the genre tags skew hard toward hip-hop (6 of the 12 tracks) even though the labels attached don't support that, and two tracks show spins recorded before their official release date.
steps.note: This is the pattern to watch for: a data quality caveat, named plainly and up front, instead of buried or quietly worked around. That's what you want it to do with your real spreadsheets too.

steps.title: Bring the ranked list to the meeting, not a decision
steps.do: Use the ranked list as your talking order in the room. The rank tells you what to discuss first — it does not tell you what to add.
steps.youWillSee: A table with every track, whether it's local, and a one-line reason it's ranked where it is — sorted so locals and time-sensitive releases surface to the top.
steps.note: Nothing in the output tells you to add anything. That's deliberate: the room decides, the list just saves you the hour of digging.

verify.check: Cross-check the local flag against your own knowledge of the artist roster.
verify.why: The 'local' flag in the source file could be wrong or out of date, and getting a local artist's priority wrong is the exact failure this walkthrough is meant to prevent.
verify.ifWrong: Correct the file yourself and re-run the prompt, or tell Claude directly which rows are actually local.

verify.check: Confirm the spin counts and release dates match what your programming system shows.
verify.why: If the sheet you exported is stale, the ranking will be built on outdated urgency — a track that already aired last week could still show up as 'time-sensitive.'
verify.ifWrong: Re-export the spreadsheet fresh from your system and run the prompt again before the meeting.

troubleshooting.symptom: Claude ranks everything the same, or the ranking doesn't make sense.
troubleshooting.cause: It's likely relying on a column that's unreliable, like a mis-tagged genre field, without you knowing.
troubleshooting.fix: Ask it directly: 'Which columns did you actually use to rank these, and which did you ignore?'

troubleshooting.symptom: It suggests what to actually add to rotation.
troubleshooting.cause: The prompt didn't draw the line clearly enough between prep and decision.
troubleshooting.fix: Remind it in the same conversation: 'Just rank and flag — the room decides what airs.' Then ask it to redo the summary without recommendations.
